file_name: PSGT Agreement NDA New Supplier Non Disclosure Template DC.txt

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall (and shall procure that each of its relevant affiliates shall), and shall use reasonable endeavors to procure that any third party subcontractors shall, comply with their respective obligations under all applicable law regarding data protection in relation to all Personal Data and Confidential Information (together, “Protected Data”) that is processed by it in the course of performing its obligations under this Agreemen t, and take such steps as are reasonably required by the other Party to ensure that the other Party is able to comply with such law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall comply with their obligations regarding data protection in relation to all Personal Data and Confidential Information (together, “Protected Data”) that is processed by it in the course of performing its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of the requirement to ensure affiliates and subcontractors comply with data protection laws weakens the overall data protection obligations and removes the explicit duty to take reasonable steps to ensure the other party can comply with data protection laws. This introduces a legal risk related to compliance with data protection regulations, specifically regarding third-party data processing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The D.C. Personal Information Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Official Code § 28-3853 requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices to protect personal information. By removing the obligation to ensure affiliates and subcontractors also comply, the agreement potentially fails to meet the 'reasonable security procedures' standard, especially when third parties are involved in processing personal data.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates that each party ensures its affiliates and subcontractors comply with data protection laws. The changed text omits this requirement. The Law Explanation suggests this omission might fail to meet the 'reasonable security procedures' standard required by D.C. Official Code § 28-3853 when third parties process personal data. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Â§ 28â3853, indicating that a violation of this section is an unfair or deceptive trade practice pursuant to Â§ 28-3904(kk) . Therefore, the omission creates a potential legal contradiction by weakening data protection obligations, potentially leading to non-compliance and thus an unfair trade practice.

perturbation.original_text: This includes maintaining a valid and up to date registration or notification under applicable law relating to data protection. “Personal Data” and “processing” and “processed” (when used in the context of PURCHASING & SUPPLY GROUP NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT: EXECUTION COPY 3 Personal Data) have the meanings given to thos e terms in the EU Data Protection Directive 95/46/EC and the applicable implementing legislat ion in any country including the Data Protection Act 1998 (the “Data Protection Laws”).
perturbation.changed_text: “Personal Data” and “processing” and “processed” (when used in the context of PURCHASING & SUPPLY GROUP NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT: EXECUTION COPY 3 Personal Data) have the meanings commonly understood in business practice.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the requirement to maintain a valid registration under applicable data protection law and the reference to the EU Data Protection Directive, the contract becomes vague and potentially non-compliant. D.C. law requires specific definitions and adherence to established data protection standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The D.C. Personal Information Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Official Code § 28-3852(a)(1) defines a 'breach of the security of the system' as unauthorized access and acquisition of unencrypted and unredacted personal information. By omitting the standard definition of 'personal data,' the agreement could be interpreted as not covering all information that is legally considered personal data, thus failing to comply with the notification requirements in case of a breach.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28â3852. Notification of security breach. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28â3852. Notification of security breach. (a) Any person or entity who conducts business in the District of Columbia, and who, in the course of such business, owns or licenses computerized or other electronic data that includes personal information, and who discovers a breach of the security of the system, shall promptly notify any District of Columbia resident whose personal information was included in the breach. The notification shall be made in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement, as provided in subsection (d) of this section, and with any measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore the reasonable integrity of the data system. (a-1) The notification required under subsection (a) of this section shall include: (1) To the extent possible, a description of the categories of information that were, or are reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, including the elements of personal information that were, or are reasonably believed to have been, acquired; (2) Contact information for the person or entity making the notification, including the business address, telephone number, and toll-free telephone number if one is maintained; (3) The toll-free telephone numbers and addresses for the major consumer reporting agencies, including a statement notifying the resident of the right to obtain a security freeze free of charge pursuant to 15 U.S.C. Â§ 1681c-1 and information how a resident may request a security freeze; and (4) The toll-free telephone numbers, addresses, and website addresses for the following entities, including a statement that an individual can obtain information from these sources about steps to take to avoid identity theft: (A) The Federal Trade Commission; and (B) The Office of the Attorney General for the Di

perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the original contract's requirement to adhere to the EU Data Protection Directive 95/46/EC and related data protection laws, replacing it with a vague reference to 'meanings commonly understood in business practice.' This omission directly contradicts the notification requirements detailed in D.C. Official Code § 28-3852(a)(1), as cited in the Law Explanation, which mandates notification of security breaches involving 'personal information.' By removing the standard definition of 'personal data,' the agreement fails to ensure compliance with breach notification requirements as required by D.C. law, as the definition of personal data is legally defined. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states the notification requirements for any entity doing business in D.C. that owns computerized data including personal information, when a breach occurs.

perturbation.original_text: CGSH acknowledges that, to the extent the Supplie r is a data processor within the meaning of the Data Protection Laws, the Supplier shall (and shal l use all reasonable endeavors to ensure that its sub-contractors shall) (i) only process Confid ential Information, including any client data, in accordance with CGSH’s instructions and (ii) bring into effect and maintain all reasonable technical and organizational measures to maintain security, prevent unauthorized or unlawful access to or processing of Personal Data, and accidental loss or destruction of, or damage to, Personal Data, having regard to the state of technological development and the cost of implementing the measures, so as to ensure a level of security a ppropriate to: (A) the harm that may result from breach of those measures; and (B) the nature of the Perso nal Data to be protected.
perturbation.changed_text: CGSH acknowledges that, to the extent the Supplier is a data processor, the Supplier shall (i) only process Confidential Information, including any client data, in accordance with CGSH’s instructions.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation to implement and maintain reasonable technical and organizational measures to protect personal data and ensure a level of security appropriate to the data and potential harm, the contract fails to provide adequate safeguards as required by data protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The D.C. Personal Information Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Official Code § 28-3853 mandates reasonable security procedures and practices. Eliminating the clause about technical and organizational measures directly contradicts this requirement, leaving personal data vulnerable and the supplier non-compliant.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text included a requirement for the Supplier to "bring into effect and maintain all reasonable technical and organizational measures" to protect personal data. The changed text removes this entire obligation. The Law Explanation states that D.C. Official Code § 28-3853 mandates reasonable security procedures and practices. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on enforcement and doesn't directly state the security mandate, the Law Explanation explicitly links the omission to a contradiction of § 28-3853. The removal of the security measures obligation directly contradicts the legal mandate for reasonable security procedures, making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: Further, the Data Protection Laws make it mand atory that CGSH obtain contractual guarantees from all third parties who will be given access to or who become aware of Protected Data during the course of their visits to the Premises in or der that they may undertake the role of a service provider. The Data Protection Laws also require CGSH to ensure they have measures in place to prevent the unauthorized or unlawful use of Pr otected Data and to prevent accidental loss, destruction or damage to any personal information. The Supplier must therefore ensure, by way of written contract between it and its employees, subcontractors and individuals visiting the Premises on the Supplier’s behalf (each a “Contractor” and, toget her, the “Contractors”), that if at any time a Contractor becomes aware of Protected Data the Su pplier or its Contractors will not (i) share this information with anyone outside of CGSH, (ii) take any information away from the Premises, and/or (iii) duplicate or copy any Protected Data.
perturbation.changed_text: The Supplier must therefore ensure that if at any time a Contractor becomes aware of Protected Data the Supplier or its Contractors will not share this information with anyone outside of CGSH.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirements for CGSH to obtain contractual guarantees and ensure preventative measures, and by omitting the restrictions on Contractors taking information from the premises or duplicating data, the agreement significantly weakens data protection safeguards for CGSH's data, and places the firm at potential legal liability. It also weakens the flow-down requirements on suppliers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The D.C. Personal Information Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Official Code § 28-3853 requires reasonable security procedures and practices. Omitting requirements that prevent contractors from taking or duplicating protected data directly undermines CGSH's ability to maintain adequate security for personal information, potentially violating the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes several key data protection safeguards that were present in the original contract, potentially violating D.C. Official Code § 28-3853, which the Law Explanation states requires reasonable security procedures and practices. The original text included requirements for contractual guarantees from third parties, preventative measures against unauthorized data use, and restrictions on contractors taking or duplicating data. The changed text removes these obligations, directly undermining the ability to maintain adequate security for personal information.
